words.definitionEn: to work together with others toward a shared goal
words.exampleEn: The two departments agreed to cooperate on the new project to save time.

words.definitionEn: to join with another person or group to do something together
words.exampleEn: The startup decided to partner with a larger company to reach more customers.

words.definitionEn: to try to win or do better than others in the same activity
words.exampleEn: Dozens of runners will compete in tomorrow's marathon.

words.definitionEn: a person or group competing with another for the same goal
words.exampleEn: The two coffee shops on the same street have become bitter rivals.

words.definitionEn: to discuss something formally in order to reach an agreement
words.exampleEn: The union tried to negotiate a higher salary for its workers.

words.definitionEn: to help two sides in a conflict find an agreement, without taking sides
words.exampleEn: A neutral counselor was asked to mediate the dispute between the neighbors.

words.definitionEn: to make an official decision that settles a disagreement between two sides
words.exampleEn: An independent judge was hired to arbitrate the contract dispute.

words.definitionEn: to give up or give way to someone else's demand or force
words.exampleEn: After hours of debate, the committee finally began to yield to public pressure.

words.definitionEn: to demand something firmly and refuse to accept any other option
words.exampleEn: She continued to insist that her account of the accident was accurate.

words.definitionEn: to continue doing something despite difficulty or opposition
words.exampleEn: Even after two failed experiments, the scientist chose to persist with her research.

words.definitionEn: to remain strong or unharmed despite a difficult force or condition
words.exampleEn: The new bridge design was tested to withstand powerful earthquakes.

words.definitionEn: to suffer through something difficult over a long period without giving up
words.exampleEn: The settlers had to endure a harsh winter with little food.

words.definitionEn: to accept or put up with something unpleasant without complaint
words.exampleEn: The manager said she would not tolerate any further lateness from her staff.

words.definitionEn: to successfully deal with or defeat a problem or difficulty
words.exampleEn: The team had to overcome a ten-point deficit to win the championship.

words.definitionEn: to deal successfully with a difficult problem or obstacle
words.exampleEn: Engineers had to surmount serious technical challenges to complete the tunnel.

words.definitionEn: to defeat or gain complete control over something by force or effort
words.exampleEn: The climbers trained for years before they finally managed to conquer the mountain.

words.definitionEn: to make a great effort to do something difficult
words.exampleEn: Many small farmers struggle to earn enough income during a drought.

words.definitionEn: to try very hard to achieve something
words.exampleEn: The company continues to strive for better customer service every year.

words.definitionEn: to try earnestly to do something
words.exampleEn: The volunteers endeavor to clean the entire beach before sunset.

words.definitionEn: to make an effort to do something, without guaranteeing success
words.exampleEn: The pilot decided to attempt a landing despite the strong winds.

words.definitionEn: to commit to and begin a task or responsibility
words.exampleEn: The city agreed to undertake a major renovation of the old library.

words.definitionEn: to follow or work toward a goal over time
words.exampleEn: After college, she decided to pursue a career in marine biology.

words.definitionEn: to complete or achieve something that was promised or expected
words.exampleEn: The contractor promised to fulfill the terms of the agreement by June.

words.definitionEn: to finish doing or making something
words.exampleEn: The workers expect to complete the new road by next spring.

words.definitionEn: to start or begin a process or action
words.exampleEn: The board voted to initiate a full review of company spending.

words.definitionEn: to begin or start, often formally
words.exampleEn: The graduation ceremony will commence at ten o'clock sharp.

words.definitionEn: to start doing or happening
words.exampleEn: The meeting will begin as soon as everyone has arrived.

words.definitionEn: to bring something to an end
words.exampleEn: The company decided to terminate the contract after repeated delays.

words.definitionEn: to complete the last details of something so it is ready
words.exampleEn: The two sides met to finalize the terms of the merger.

words.definitionEn: to keep doing something without stopping
words.exampleEn: Despite the rain, the players decided to continue the match.

words.definitionEn: to go forward or continue with an action after a pause or decision
words.exampleEn: Once the permits were approved, the builders were free to proceed with construction.

words.definitionEn: to stop a process or activity for a short time by breaking into it
words.exampleEn: A sudden power outage interrupted the live broadcast.

words.definitionEn: to stop something temporarily, intending to start it again later
words.exampleEn: The airline had to suspend flights during the storm.

words.definitionEn: to stop moving or happening, often suddenly
words.exampleEn: Construction came to a halt when workers discovered ancient ruins underground.

words.definitionEn: to begin again after a pause or interruption
words.exampleEn: Talks between the two countries are set to resume next week.

words.definitionEn: to start something again from the beginning or after stopping
words.exampleEn: Technicians had to restart the entire system after it crashed.

words.definitionEn: to begin something again or extend it for a further period
words.exampleEn: She had to renew her passport before traveling abroad.

words.definitionEn: to become progressively worse
words.exampleEn: The patient's condition began to deteriorate overnight.

words.definitionEn: to gradually become smaller, weaker, or worse
words.exampleEn: Sales began to decline sharply after the competitor entered the market.

words.definitionEn: to move forward or make progress
words.exampleEn: Medical research continues to advance at a remarkable pace.

words.definitionEn: to develop or improve over time
words.exampleEn: The student's writing skills progressed quickly after weekly practice.

words.definitionEn: to return to a worse or less developed state
words.exampleEn: Without practice, his language skills began to regress.

words.definitionEn: to become or make something more bad or severe
words.exampleEn: Traffic conditions tend to worsen during the holiday season.

words.definitionEn: to stop developing or making progress
words.exampleEn: The local economy began to stagnate after the factory closed.

words.definitionEn: to stop making progress temporarily
words.exampleEn: Negotiations between the two companies stalled over pricing disagreements.

words.definitionEn: to reach a stable level after a period of increase
words.exampleEn: Her weight loss plateaued after the first two months of the diet.

words.definitionEn: to succeed and become financially or generally strong
words.exampleEn: Small businesses began to prosper once the new highway opened.

words.definitionEn: to grow or develop very well and successfully
words.exampleEn: The garden thrives thanks to the rich soil and steady rainfall.

words.definitionEn: to grow, develop, or succeed vigorously
words.exampleEn: The arts scene in the city has flourished over the last decade.

words.definitionEn: to rot or gradually break down and lose strength
words.exampleEn: The abandoned building began to decay after years of neglect.

words.definitionEn: to gradually decrease in strength, size, or importance
words.exampleEn: Public interest in the trend began to wane after a few months.

words.definitionEn: to become visible or come into existence
words.exampleEn: A rainbow appeared over the valley after the storm passed.

words.definitionEn: to come up to the top or become known after being hidden
words.exampleEn: New evidence surfaced years after the original investigation closed.

words.definitionEn: to stop being visible or to cease existing
words.exampleEn: The hikers watched the sun disappear behind the mountains.

words.definitionEn: to disappear suddenly and completely
words.exampleEn: The magician made the coin vanish in front of the audience.

words.definitionEn: to gradually become less visible, bright, or strong
words.exampleEn: The old photograph had faded so much that the faces were hard to see.

words.definitionEn: to stay in the same place or condition without changing
words.exampleEn: Despite the criticism, the mayor chose to remain in office.

words.definitionEn: to stop happening or existing
words.exampleEn: The factory ceased production after the fire damaged its equipment.

words.definitionEn: to no longer continue an action or movement
words.exampleEn: The bus stopped suddenly to avoid hitting a dog in the road.

words.definitionEn: to stop making, producing, or providing something
words.exampleEn: The company decided to discontinue the unpopular product line.

words.definitionEn: to put something into use for a particular purpose
words.exampleEn: Students were asked to apply the formula to solve the problem.

words.definitionEn: to make practical use of something
words.exampleEn: The team decided to utilize satellite data to track the storm.

words.definitionEn: to use a particular method, skill, or object for a purpose
words.exampleEn: The author employs vivid imagery to describe the battlefield.

words.definitionEn: to take or employ something for a specific purpose
words.exampleEn: She learned to use the new software within a single afternoon.

words.definitionEn: to use a power, right, or quality actively
words.exampleEn: The judge chose to exercise caution before issuing a final ruling.

words.definitionEn: to choose a course of action after considering options
words.exampleEn: The committee will decide tomorrow whether to approve the budget.

words.definitionEn: to suggest an explanation based on limited evidence, as a starting point for investigation
words.exampleEn: Scientists hypothesize that the disease spreads through contaminated water.

words.definitionEn: to form a theory or general explanation about something
words.exampleEn: Researchers theorize that the ancient city was destroyed by an earthquake.

words.definitionEn: to check that something is true or accurate
words.exampleEn: The editor asked the reporter to verify every fact before publishing the story.

words.definitionEn: to state or show that something is true or correct
words.exampleEn: The lab results confirmed the doctor's initial diagnosis.

words.definitionEn: to prove or officially recognize that something is accurate or legitimate
words.exampleEn: The new experiment helped validate the scientist's original theory.

words.definitionEn: to show that something is false
words.exampleEn: The new fossil evidence helped disprove the earlier theory about the species.

words.definitionEn: to state the opposite of something, showing it is false or inconsistent
words.exampleEn: The witness's testimony seemed to contradict the earlier police report.

words.definitionEn: to accept or admit that something is true or exists
words.exampleEn: The company was forced to acknowledge the defect in its product.

words.definitionEn: to confess that something, often unfavorable, is true
words.exampleEn: He finally admitted that he had made a mistake on the report.

words.definitionEn: to state that something is not true
words.exampleEn: The official continued to deny any involvement in the scandal.

words.definitionEn: to indicate that one is not willing to do or accept something
words.exampleEn: The employee refused to sign the new contract without changes.

words.definitionEn: to agree to receive or take something offered
words.exampleEn: She decided to accept the job offer after much thought.

words.definitionEn: to accept something willingly and enthusiastically
words.exampleEn: The town chose to embrace new technology to modernize its schools.

words.definitionEn: to receive someone or something with pleasure or approval
words.exampleEn: The committee welcomed the new proposal as a step in the right direction.

words.definitionEn: unwilling and hesitant to do something
words.exampleEn: He was reluctant to share his opinion in front of the whole class.

words.definitionEn: slow to act or speak because of uncertainty
words.exampleEn: She felt hesitant about signing the contract without reading it fully.

words.definitionEn: not wanting to do something
words.exampleEn: The witness was unwilling to answer any further questions.

words.definitionEn: ready and happy to do something
words.exampleEn: Several volunteers were willing to stay late and help clean up.

words.definitionEn: having a strong desire or interest to do something
words.exampleEn: The new intern was eager to learn every part of the business.

words.definitionEn: showing intense excitement and interest in something
words.exampleEn: The coach was enthusiastic about the team's improvement this season.

words.definitionEn: not emotionally involved or interested in something
words.exampleEn: He remained detached during the meeting, offering no opinion either way.

words.definitionEn: worried or interested about a particular matter
words.exampleEn: Parents grew concerned about the safety of the playground equipment.

words.definitionEn: deeply interested in and committed to something
words.exampleEn: The players were fully invested in winning the championship this year.

words.definitionEn: actively involved and interested in something
words.exampleEn: The students stayed engaged throughout the entire two-hour lecture.

words.definitionEn: having doubts about whether a claim is true
words.exampleEn: Many scientists remained skeptical of the company's bold claims.

words.definitionEn: feeling uncertain about whether something is true or will happen
words.exampleEn: Investors were doubtful that the small company could repay its debt.

words.definitionEn: feeling sure about one's abilities or about an outcome
words.exampleEn: The coach felt confident that the team would win the final match.

words.definitionEn: having no doubt that something is true
words.exampleEn: She was certain she had left her keys on the kitchen table.

words.definitionEn: confident and free from self-doubt
words.exampleEn: The lawyer spoke in an assured tone throughout the closing argument.

words.definitionEn: not known or definite; lacking confidence
words.exampleEn: The future of the old factory remains uncertain after the announcement.

words.definitionEn: hopeful and confident about the future
words.exampleEn: Despite the setback, the team stayed optimistic about the season ahead.

words.definitionEn: feeling or expressing a positive expectation about the future
words.exampleEn: Doctors remain hopeful that the new treatment will help patients recover faster.

words.definitionEn: expecting the worst possible outcome
words.exampleEn: Analysts grew pessimistic about the company's chances of survival.

words.definitionEn: offering little hope or encouragement; grim
words.exampleEn: The report painted a bleak picture of the region's economic future.

quizzes.passage: The formal proceedings will commence promptly at nine, and latecomers will not be permitted to enter until the first recess.
quizzes.question: As used in the passage, 'commence' most nearly means
quizzes.explanation: The strict starting time and the mention of latecomers indicate that 'commence' means to formally begin.

quizzes.passage: Left exposed to the weather, the wooden fence began to decay, its boards softening and crumbling within a few short years.
quizzes.question: As used in the passage, 'decay' most nearly means
quizzes.explanation: The softening and crumbling boards describe the process of rotting, which is the meaning of 'decay.'

quizzes.passage: Strange lights began to appear over the desert each night, drawing curious tourists and confused scientists alike.
quizzes.question: As used in the passage, 'appear' most nearly means
quizzes.explanation: Lights becoming visible each night over the desert is the meaning of 'appear.'

quizzes.passage: When the two companies could not settle their contract dispute privately, they hired an independent arbitrator whose decision, unlike a mediator's suggestion, would be legally binding on both parties.
quizzes.question: As used in the passage, 'arbitrate' most nearly means

options.t: to formally settle a dispute with a binding decision
to end all communication between two sides

quizzes.explanation: The passage contrasts arbitration's binding decision with mediation's non-binding suggestion, showing arbitrate means to formally settle with authority.

quizzes.passage: The report painted a bleak picture of the fishing industry, warning that overfishing could collapse the region's economy within a decade.
quizzes.question: Which choice best states the main idea of the passage?

options.t: Overfishing has already been fully resolved.
The report focused only on unrelated industries.
The fishing industry is thriving beyond expectations.
The fishing industry has a discouraging and troubled outlook.

quizzes.explanation: The warning of economic collapse from overfishing conveys a grim, discouraging outlook, matching 'bleak.'

quizzes.passage: By the third season, fans were fully invested in the characters' lives, discussing plot theories online for hours after each episode.
quizzes.question: As used in the passage, 'invested' most nearly means
quizzes.explanation: Fans discussing theories for hours shows deep interest and commitment, the meaning of 'invested' in this context.

quizzes.passage: After a strong first quarter, the merger negotiations stalled when both companies disagreed sharply over how to value certain assets.
quizzes.question: As used in the passage, 'stall' most nearly means
quizzes.explanation: The disagreement halting progress in the negotiations is precisely what 'stall' describes.

quizzes.passage: New fossil discoveries helped disprove the long-held belief that the species had gone extinct thousands of years earlier than it actually did.
quizzes.question: As used in the passage, 'disprove' most nearly means

options.t: to show that a claim is false

quizzes.explanation: The fossils showing the earlier belief was wrong is the meaning of 'disprove.'

quizzes.passage: Though offered the promotion, she remained reluctant, worried that the added responsibility would leave no time for her family.
quizzes.question: As used in the passage, 'reluctant' most nearly means
quizzes.explanation: Her worry and unwillingness to accept the promotion demonstrate the meaning of 'reluctant.'

quizzes.passage: Engineers apply principles of physics learned in the classroom to solve real construction problems on the job site.
quizzes.question: As used in the passage, 'apply' most nearly means
quizzes.explanation: Using classroom knowledge to solve real problems demonstrates 'apply,' putting something into practical use.

quizzes.passage: When the two departments could not agree on a budget, management brought in a neutral facilitator to mediate. Unlike a judge who decides a case, the facilitator only helped both sides communicate until they reached their own agreement.
quizzes.question: Which choice best describes the function of 'mediate' in the passage?

options.t: It refers to imposing a binding legal ruling.
It refers to punishing one side for wrongdoing.
It refers to ending the negotiation permanently.
It refers to helping two sides reach their own agreement without taking sides.

quizzes.explanation: The passage explicitly contrasts a mediator, who helps sides reach their own agreement, with a judge who decides for them.

quizzes.passage: Thanks to fertile soil and abundant rain, the region's farms have long prospered, exporting crops across the entire continent.
quizzes.question: As used in the passage, 'prosper' most nearly means
quizzes.explanation: The farms exporting crops widely due to good conditions shows they succeeded and grew, the meaning of 'prosper.'

quizzes.passage: The festival will initiate a series of cultural events that continue through the entire month, beginning with a parade and ending with a closing concert.
quizzes.question: As used in the passage, 'initiate' most nearly means

options.t: to conclude a series of events

quizzes.explanation: The passage describes the festival beginning with a parade, which matches 'initiate' meaning to start something.

quizzes.passage: Before publishing the story, the journalist spent days trying to verify each claim made by her anonymous source.
quizzes.question: As used in the passage, 'verify' most nearly means

options.t: to check the accuracy of information

quizzes.explanation: Checking each claim before publishing demonstrates 'verify,' confirming that information is accurate.

quizzes.passage: Rather than compete for the same shrinking customer base, the two small bakeries decided to cooperate, sharing suppliers and splitting the cost of advertising. Within a year, both shops saw higher profits than they had as separate rivals.
quizzes.question: As used in the passage, 'cooperate' most nearly means

options.t: to work jointly toward a common benefit

quizzes.explanation: The passage shows the bakeries sharing suppliers and advertising costs instead of competing, which is the meaning of 'cooperate.'

quizzes.passage: The witness was unwilling to testify publicly, fearing retaliation from the people she would be accusing.
quizzes.question: As used in the passage, 'unwilling' most nearly means
quizzes.explanation: The witness's fear-driven refusal to testify shows she was not wanting to act, the meaning of 'unwilling.'

quizzes.passage: Left without new investment for years, the small town's economy began to stagnate, with no new jobs or businesses appearing.
quizzes.question: As used in the passage, 'stagnate' most nearly means
quizzes.explanation: The absence of new jobs or businesses shows the economy stopped developing, the meaning of 'stagnate.'

quizzes.passage: In her speech, the senator employed powerful rhetorical questions to persuade the audience of the bill's urgency.
quizzes.question: As used in the passage, 'employ' most nearly means

options.t: to make use of a method or technique

quizzes.explanation: Using rhetorical questions as a technique to persuade shows the meaning of 'employ' here, to make use of a method.

quizzes.passage: Down by twenty points at halftime, the underdog team seemed finished. Yet through relentless defense and a string of late three-pointers, they managed to overcome the deficit and win in overtime.
quizzes.question: Which choice best states the main idea of the passage?

options.t: The game ended in a tie.
The team came back from a large deficit to win.
The team lost despite a strong effort.
The team gave up once they fell behind.

quizzes.explanation: The passage traces the team's comeback from a twenty-point deficit to an overtime win, which is the main idea.

quizzes.passage: The new coach was enthusiastic from day one, organizing extra practices and cheering loudly at every small improvement.
quizzes.question: As used in the passage, 'enthusiastic' most nearly means
quizzes.explanation: The coach's extra effort and loud encouragement demonstrate strong excitement, the meaning of 'enthusiastic.'

quizzes.passage: Under the new mayor's policies, small businesses have flourished downtown, filling storefronts that had been empty for years.
quizzes.question: As used in the passage, 'flourish' most nearly means
quizzes.explanation: Businesses filling previously empty storefronts shows vigorous growth, matching the meaning of 'flourish.'

quizzes.passage: Having trained for years, the gymnast felt confident stepping onto the mat for the final routine of her career.
quizzes.question: As used in the passage, 'confident' most nearly means
quizzes.explanation: Years of training leading to a feeling of readiness matches the meaning of 'confident.'

quizzes.passage: After reviewing the audit, the executives had to acknowledge that the company's spending had far exceeded its budget for three consecutive years.
quizzes.question: As used in the passage, 'acknowledge' most nearly means

options.t: to accept that something is true

quizzes.explanation: Accepting the audit's findings about overspending is the meaning of 'acknowledge.'

quizzes.passage: The future of the abandoned mill remains uncertain, with the city still debating whether to restore it or tear it down.
quizzes.question: As used in the passage, 'uncertain' most nearly means
quizzes.explanation: The city still debating the mill's fate shows the outcome is not yet known, the meaning of 'uncertain.'

quizzes.passage: Renewable energy technology continues to advance rapidly, with solar panel efficiency nearly doubling over the past decade.
quizzes.question: As used in the passage, 'advance' most nearly means
quizzes.explanation: The doubling of solar efficiency over a decade illustrates 'advance,' meaning to move forward or improve.

quizzes.passage: Based on unusual patterns in ocean temperature, researchers hypothesize that a previously unknown current shapes weather along the coast.
quizzes.question: As used in the passage, 'hypothesize' most nearly means
quizzes.explanation: Proposing an explanation based on unusual patterns, without proof, is the meaning of 'hypothesize.'

quizzes.passage: By royal decree, all hostilities between the two kingdoms were ordered to cease immediately, ending a war that had lasted a decade.
quizzes.question: As used in the passage, 'cease' most nearly means
quizzes.explanation: Ending a decade-long war by decree shows hostilities were ordered to stop, the meaning of 'cease.'

quizzes.passage: Without treatment, the untreated wound began to deteriorate, growing more infected and painful with each passing day.
quizzes.question: As used in the passage, 'deteriorate' most nearly means
quizzes.explanation: The wound growing more infected and painful over time shows 'deteriorate' means to progressively get worse.

quizzes.passage: After fulfilling every requirement of her apprenticeship, the young carpenter was finally allowed to complete her certification and open her own workshop.
quizzes.question: As used in the passage, 'fulfill' most nearly means
quizzes.explanation: Meeting every requirement of the apprenticeship shows 'fulfill' means to satisfy or complete something owed or promised.

quizzes.passage: Despite the recent losses, the CEO remained optimistic, telling employees that the company's long-term outlook was still strong.
quizzes.question: As used in the passage, 'optimistic' most nearly means
quizzes.explanation: The CEO's positive message about the future despite losses matches the meaning of 'optimistic.'

quizzes.passage: Economists remained skeptical of the report's rosy projections, noting that similar forecasts had proven wildly inaccurate in the past.
quizzes.question: As used in the passage, 'skeptical' most nearly means
quizzes.explanation: Economists doubting the projections because of past inaccuracy is the meaning of 'skeptical.'

quizzes.passage: Under intense questioning, the suspect finally admitted that he had been at the scene, though he still denied taking anything.
quizzes.question: As used in the passage, 'admit' most nearly means
quizzes.explanation: Confessing to being at the scene, even while denying theft, is the meaning of 'admit.'

quizzes.passage: As the fog rolled in, the lighthouse seemed to disappear entirely, leaving sailors to navigate by sound alone.
quizzes.question: As used in the passage, 'disappear' most nearly means
quizzes.explanation: The lighthouse becoming invisible in the fog matches the meaning of 'disappear.'

quizzes.passage: The two rival tech firms, once fierce rivals racing to release the same product, eventually chose to partner on a joint venture that combined their strengths.
quizzes.question: Which choice best describes the relationship shift described in the passage?
quizzes.explanation: The passage traces the firms moving from being rivals to becoming partners, i.e., from competition to cooperation.

quizzes.passage: Once the region's main employer closed its factory, local business began to decline, and many families moved elsewhere in search of work.
quizzes.question: Which choice best supports the claim that the factory's closing hurt the local economy?
quizzes.explanation: The phrase directly states that business activity worsened, which is the clearest evidence of economic harm.

quizzes.passage: A loud alarm suddenly interrupted the lecture, forcing the professor to pause mid-sentence and evacuate the building.
quizzes.question: As used in the passage, 'interrupt' most nearly means

options.t: to break into and stop an ongoing activity

quizzes.explanation: The alarm forcing the professor to stop mid-sentence is a clear example of 'interrupt,' breaking into an activity.

quizzes.passage: Over the decades, the ink on the old letters began to fade, until entire paragraphs became nearly impossible to read.
quizzes.question: As used in the passage, 'fade' most nearly means

options.t: to gradually lose visibility or strength

quizzes.explanation: The ink becoming harder to read over decades shows a gradual loss of visibility, the meaning of 'fade.'

quizzes.passage: Due to safety concerns after the derailment, officials chose to suspend train service on the northern line until inspectors completed a full review.
quizzes.question: As used in the passage, 'suspend' most nearly means

options.t: to temporarily stop, intending to resume later

quizzes.explanation: The service was stopped only until the review was complete, matching 'suspend,' a temporary halt intended to resume.
